## [03:03 - 06:07]

So if you're not paying for the products, you are the product. And I think this is something which people don't understand when they're using Facebook. They're using it for free. Facebook cannot operate without money. They've got servers, they've got people, they've got other things to pay for. And essentially, since they're not charging you to access Facebook, they need to find another way. And that's where monetization comes through in ads. In order to make the ads as best as they can, they use your data as a user to bring that to advertisers and hopefully allow the advertiser and the consumer to have a match between a product or a service that the user finds useful. And obviously, the revenue numbers that Facebook are pulling through ads tells you that that's working. However, when it comes to data privacy, the whole Cambridge Analytica thing is blown it up for Facebook. And it's given Facebook a far bigger focus than they've ever had before. Now, if you go back many years ago, data and privacy has always been a concern for big companies. So if you think about Google, Amazon, Microsoft companies like that, Google in particular, I did a post about this on my profile a few weeks ago, Google actually have worryingly more data and quite kind of sensitive data on you than Facebook does. So for example, let's say you've got medical conditioning, you just search for it. Well, guess what Google know about that. So, you know, if you just think about all the things you're searching for in Google and what kind of profile they're building up with you, there's stuff there that Facebook doesn't even have. Then you take it to another level where Amazon actually have far more data than people realize, because not only do they run an e-commerce operation and know exactly what you're buying and when, but they also have their technology in the background, Amazon Web Services, which manages like hundreds of thousands of other websites and they're collecting data about you from all these sites. So it's not just Facebook that are responsible for data, it's all these big companies. So GDPR stands for General Data Protection Regulation and what regulation means is it's putting some controls over what's out there. So data protection has been there for decades and consumer rights and things like that. But now we're in the digital age, that's where the regulations are coming into catch up with where we are right now and that's really where this comes from. And this is something which users, I don't think have been properly educated on, that ads will still be served in Facebook, but the relevancy will be massively reduced if they block that data going into Facebook. What it's trying to do is to try and rebuild trust in big companies and data, trying to increase the transparency of the data. And for you, it's about avoiding business loss. So these are the things that you need to kind of focus on. So three of the three things you need to do is give control, ask for consent and be explicit on that concern as well. So this was the kind of process that they created internally on how to manage privacy within Facebook. What the GDPR is trying to do is to make sure that we are all following a similar process where privacy and data protection is a core part of your business for too long. It's been an afterthought. So people have started businesses, entrepreneurs, where it is, and then data and privacy is just bolted on either when there's a problem or when there's a technical person that comes in and says, hey, what are you doing with this? You know, CSV file of 100,000 customers are just sitting in this public drive. So that what GDPR is trying to get people to focus on is privacy is actually part of the core part of your business. And also the data controller takes the first point of consent. So I'll explain the controller versus processor just in case you don't know, but the controller is the person who is actually taking that data and the processor processes it. And there's different levels of conformance to make sure that you're handling that properly as well. So when it comes to Facebook, Facebook is a data controller for collecting data. So when it's collecting the pixel data and tracking data and all that kind of stuff off your website or your app or your social login, any tools that Facebook offer you, Facebook is a data controller. What that means is they are sucking that data out and they're pulling that data and they're choosing what to pull out. Facebook is a data processor also for the same data. However, when it comes to custom audiences, analytics and also workplace by Facebook, Facebook is not the data controller. So with custom audiences, you are giving the data, for example, your emails to Facebook or your mobile numbers or whatever it is.

So it includes a right to access, right to be forgotten, data transparency, personal data. So personal data is anything identifiable to that individual person. The processing of the data, whether it's manual or automated, and also how the records are kept and how people can get access to that as well. Then kind of difference between subject, controller and processor. A subject is the consumer or the customer, the person whose data it belongs to. The controller is the person collecting it and the processor is the person or entity that's actually doing something with the data. So if a customer gives you an email address, they're the subject, you're the controller because you collect an email. Then let's say you put it into a custom audience, then Facebook is your data processor. And so that's how that setup works. In terms of compliance, make sure your privacy terms are in plain English. From my preference, make sure it's suitable for an 11 to 12 year old to read it and make sense of it. If there's a data breach, make sure that you notify your customers within 72 hours. Ideally, the recommendation says without undue delays. So if there's a data breach, you have to have a process in place. Make sure that you've got that process written out. So if any of your customers' data is, for example, your clients or your own customers, whether you're running an e-commerce or whatever, if any of that data is breached, then you make sure you let them know ASAP and allow them to take action with NEP. The cost of breaching the GDPR is 4% of your annual global turnover of 20 million euros, which isn't a small amount because it's turnover not profit as well.
